passages.topic_jp: 独身でいる理由（アンケート）
passages.summary_en: A survey on reasons people stay single found 'haven't met the right person' as the top answer. The writer reflects on their own outlook: they hope to meet someone eventually but don't feel pressure to marry soon; they understand their parents may worry, but feel the timing of marriage is a personal choice, not something others should decide for them.

passages.topic_jp: 育児休業を取った男性の体験談
passages.summary_en: A father describes telling his boss he'd take paternity leave once told his wife was pregnant; his workplace and colleagues were supportive. He wanted dedicated time with his newborn. He took about 2 months of leave during his wife's postpartum leave -- under the system, both parents can't take leave simultaneously, so he handed off to his wife afterward. He helped with everything except breastfeeding, sometimes struggling when the baby cried from hunger and he couldn't feed him. Now back at work, he tries to leave early and finds renewed motivation seeing his son grow.

passages.topic_jp: お酒・タバコの我慢についての意見（A/B）
passages.summary_en: Two opinions on restraint around smoking/drinking for health. A: forcing yourself to abstain from things you enjoy just causes stress and is pointless -- enjoy them as an occasional treat. B: excessive abstinence is bad for mental health (cites a grandfather who smoked and lived to 95); disagrees that smoking has zero benefits; not all smokers/drinkers get cancer -- enjoying them without bothering others is fine.

Explains the body's internal clock runs ~25 hours, an hour off from the 24-hour day; eating breakfast resets it daily. Skipping breakfast causes jet-lag-like symptoms. People with no morning appetite should drink something right after waking; people short on time can prep frozen rice balls/bread the night before. Eating late at night promotes fat storage, kills next-morning appetite, and delays bedtime, preventing proper rest -- so eat dinner early, sleep enough, and eat a proper breakfast for a regular rhythm.

passages.topic_jp: NARUTOファンの留学生のブログ
passages.summary_en: A Russian university student majoring in Japanese culture, on short-term exchange in Japan, loves the manga NARUTO -- especially the protagonist pursuing a big dream alongside his friends without giving up. Manga was popular among friends back home, which motivated coming to Japan; now they've met many fellow fans, and the happiest part is being able to get desired manga right away, eagerly attending related events for courage and energy.

Explains TABLE FOR TWO (TFT): companies/universities offer low-calorie healthy meals, adding 20 yen donated to provide a meal for a child in Africa via NPOs -- linking the ~1 billion in developed countries with lifestyle diseases/obesity to a similar number suffering from hunger in developing countries. Started 2007 by Kogure Masahisa; spread via recipe books, convenience stores, family restaurants, even wedding catering. Kogure highlights children's happy eating as the true measure of success; the initiative has spread internationally (US, other Asian countries), connecting people through a small, accessible 20-yen contribution.

About 400 years ago, Edo (now Tokyo) was denser than Paris or London at the time, with people from many regions living together. Living wisdom developed to avoid friction among people of different backgrounds sharing tight space -- called 'Edo shigusa.' Examples: 'kasa-kashige' (umbrella tilting) -- when passing someone in the rain, both tilt umbrellas outward to avoid dripping on each other; and 'shichi-san aruki' (7:3 walking) -- road width split 3:7, ordinary walkers use the narrow 3 portion, leaving the wider 7 open for people in a hurry or emergencies, originating from giving way to messengers (hikyaku).

passages.topic_jp: 「すみません」についての投書（タニアさん・中川さん）
passages.summary_en: Two reader-letter passages. A: Tania, an exchange student from Thailand working part-time at a convenience store, describes a customer's basket knocking goods off a shelf, and her senior coworker apologizing ('sumimasen') even though the customer caused it -- she finds this puzzling. B: Nakagawa, a housewife, shares a similar experience at a clothing shop where a blouse fell off a hanger and the clerk apologized and fixed it -- she felt this showed attentive customer care, suggesting the convenience-store clerk's apology came from the same spirit.

passages.topic_jp: ステマ（ステルスマーケティング）
passages.summary_en: Explains 'stema' (stealth marketing): promoting something without it being recognized as an ad, influencing not just the listener but bystanders too. It has spread quietly with the internet, including celebrities casually endorsing sponsored products, prompting fans to buy and spread it further -- exploiting fan psychology. Consumers may think they're choosing freely but may be influenced by someone else's intent; since SNS spreads information instantly, critical evaluation of information and responsibility for what one shares both matter.

passages.topic_jp: 石巻日日新聞の手書き壁新聞
passages.summary_en: Tells the story of the Ishinomaki Hibi Shimbun's handwritten wall newspapers after the March 11, 2011 earthquake/tsunami. The building flooded and lost power, making normal printing impossible, but reporters -- themselves disaster victims with unconfirmed family safety -- decided that with just paper and a pen they could keep informing people. They gathered info on foot from city hall, police, and shelters, prioritizing practical needs (shelter locations, supplies, road conditions), believing information is a lifeline like water or electricity. From day three, headlines shifted toward hopeful news as reporters realized information could give people hope. The papers were posted at 6 shelters for 6 days until power returned, later gained international attention via the Washington Post, were preserved at the Newseum in Washington D.C., and exhibited in Paris.

passages.topic_jp: 学校の掃除についての意見（A・たわしさん・ちゃおさん・レイさん）
passages.summary_en: Three short opinions on whether students should clean their own school: a middle schooler questions why students (not professional cleaners) do it; a company employee says cleaning is itself education, cleansing the heart too; a university student says it's natural to clean what you use, like your own room. 'A' then argues professional cleaners might do better -- understanding the 'responsibility for what you use' view, but doubting kids actually get things clean (some work hard, some goof off, takes too long) and noting some areas are unsafe for kids; A feels if adults clean thoroughly, kids can focus comfortably on studying and might naturally want to keep the classroom tidy themselves.

passages.topic_jp: 「16歳の君に聞きたいことがある」
passages.summary_en: An essay addressed to a 16-year-old, questioning why they're studying and planning to go to university. It challenges reasons like 'parents/teachers say so,' 'friends are going,' or 'a degree is needed for work' as merely 'reasons external to yourself,' urging the reader to think for themselves. It notes many highly accomplished people never finished university. It argues teens are largely studying 'because everyone else does,' unlike adults, who study only what's directly useful for work (businesspeople study marketing, lawyers study law) and rarely study broad subjects irrelevant to daily work. The essay challenges the reader to seriously consider, while there's still time, why they study and what the true purpose of studying is for them.

passages.topic_jp: 日本企業が留学生に求めるもの
passages.summary_en: Advises understanding what Japanese companies look for via materials, briefings, and seminars. Lists reasons companies hire international students: gaining talented people regardless of nationality, expanding overseas business, needing foreign language ability, and wanting perspectives different from Japanese employees. Companies have some concerns (communication with coworkers, risk of quitting soon) but also positive impressions (motivation, broad perspective, language skill, specialized knowledge, cross-cultural understanding) -- students should understand these and actively self-promote.

passages.topic_jp: 里山を守る活動に携わる筆者の体験談
passages.summary_en: The writer recalls being active in a college event-planning club, then joining an event-planning company after graduating, expecting to use that skill -- but in the real job, budget and client wishes took priority over the writer's own ideas, leading to quitting after 3 years. They next joined a food manufacturer in sales, but again felt unsuited -- until placed in a newly created CSR/'social contribution' department, where their company's project involved protecting 'satoyama' (rural woodland near settlements, once communally maintained but now neglected). Through farmer interviews, tree planting, grass cutting, and logging, the writer grew close with local villagers and began bringing city kids to experience satoyama. What began hesitantly became deeply rewarding -- the writer concludes a perfect-fit job isn't easy to find, but throwing yourself into whatever work comes your way can make it become irreplaceable.
